, 
State o\~ CaS:fornia 
Memorandum 
To 
Advice Request Participants 
Date 
April 25, 1983 
No. M-83-116 
From 
: 
PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Milman 
Subject: 
of Loans on SEIls 
In response to the question regarding disclosure of loans 
used for political purposes on SEI's (April 13, 1983, Advice 
Request Meeting, memo attached) we agreed: 
(1) 
Loans which are contributions do not need to be 
reported on the candidate's SEI if they are reported on the 
campaign statement. 
Section 82030(b) (1) specifically excludes 
contributions from income. 
(2) 
Loans which are not contributions must be reported on 
both the campaign statemenr-and the candidate's SEI if the 
candidate is personally liable on the loan. 
(3) 
Repayment of tne loan by the campaign committee, 
whether made directly to the lender or indirectly (to the 
candidate who repays the lender), need not be reported on the 
c and ida te 's SE I. 
(4) 
When the loan is paid off, that should be shown on the 
SEI (if the loan was disclosed on the SEI because it was not a 
contribution) • 
(5) 
A loan is not a contribution (Section 84216) if it is 
made by a commercial-rending institution in the ordinary course 
of business. 
All other loans (i.e., loans from private lenders, 
loans at below market interest, unsecured loans etc.) are 
presumed to be contributions, unless it is clear from the 
surrounding circumstances that the loan was not made for 
political purpose. 
It will usually not be possible to show that 
a private loan was not made for'political purposes. 
However, it 
was recognized that in some instances it may be possible to 
demonstrate that a loan from a private lender was not made for 
political purposes (i.e., from a private lender who is in the 
business of making loans, and makes the loan as an arms-length 
transaction in the ordinary course of his business), or that a 
loan from a bank without collateral was not made for political 


Advice Request Participants 
April 25, 1983 
Page 2 
purposes (if the bank ordinarily makes such loans to good 
customers or to get new business). 
Anyone asking advice about a 
loan, other than one from a commercial lending institution in the 
ordinary course of business, who thinks the loan may not be a 
contribution, should probably be referred to Legal for-advice. 
(6) 
For purposes of reporting and disclosure, whenever a 
candidate is personally liable on a loan which is not a 
contribution (he signs for the loan, the loan is made directly to 
him, or his property is used for security for the loan), the loan 
is income and must be reported on the SEI.

Reporting LoanS on Statements of Economic Interests 
An officeholder/candidate gets a loan for his campaign. 
He puts 
up his own personal property as collateral, and signs for the loan 
personally. 
Must he report the outstanding loan on his SEI when: 
(1) 
The loan is from a private party; the check from the lender 
is payable to the candidate; the candidate turns over the proceeds to 
his campaign committee; 
(2) 
The loan is from a bank, in the ordinary course of 
business; the check from the: bank is payable to the candidate; the 
candidate turns over the proceeds to his campaign committee; 
(3) 
The loan is from a private party; the check from the lender 
is payable direc~ly to the cowmitteei 
(4) 
The loan is from a bank in the ordinary course of business; 
the check from the bank is payable directly to the committee? 
If the committee repays the loan, should the repayment (interest 
or principal) be reported on the candidate's SEI when: 
(1) 
The committee repays the lender directly; 
(2) 
The committee gives the money to the candidate who uses it 
to repay the lender? 
How does the answer to the first question (whether to report the 
outstanding loan on the SEI) affect the answer to the second question 
(whether to report repayments on the SEI)? 
Look at sections: 
82030(a) -
income includes an outstanding loan; 
82030(b) -
income does not include campaign contributions; 
84216 - loans as contributions; reporting loans which are 
not contributions. 


State of C4lJfomia 
Memoran 
March 2, 1983 
Advice Request Meeting Participants 
FROM: 
Alice Hughes 
I. 
If an individual who is not a candidate, but who is in a 
position where he must file a Form 721 or 730 (e.g., a 
Legisla ti ve aide) : 
(a) 
Makes a loan to the campaign committee, and the loan is 
later repaid by the committee, must the individual report the 
repayment of the loan on his/her SEI as income? 
(b) 
Makes expenditures on behalf of the campaign which are 
later reimbursed by the campaign committee, must the individual 
report the reimbursements as income on his/her SEI? 
II. 
If a candidate/official: 
(a) 
Makes a loan to his campaign committee, and the loan is 
later repaid by the committeeI' must the repayment be r:eported as 
income on his/her SEI? 
(b) 
Makes expenditures on behalf of his campaign which are 
later reimbursed by the campaign committee, must the 
reimbursement be reported on the SEI as income? 
Please review the attached opinion and advice letter.

Specifically, must loans be reported on the SEI when: (1) the loan is from a private party with check payable to the candidate who turns proceeds to campaign committee; (2) the loan is from a bank in ordinary course of business with check payable to the candidate; (3) the loan is from a private party with check payable directly to the committee; (4) the loan is from a bank with check payable directly to the committee? If the committee repays the loan, should repayment be reported on the candidate's SEI when: (1) the committee repays the lender directly; (2) the committee gives money to the candidate who repays the lender? How does whether the outstanding loan must be reported on the SEI affect whether repayments must be reported?

sections.question_synthetic: Must candidates report outstanding loans used for campaign purposes on their Statements of Economic Interest, and how should loan repayments be reported depending on whether the original loan was a contribution or not?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Loans that are contributions need not be reported on a candidate's SEI if reported on the campaign statement, but non-contribution loans must be reported on both documents if the candidate is personally liable. Loan repayments by the campaign committee need not be reported on the SEI, and commercial lending institution loans in the ordinary course of business are not presumed to be contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains multiple memoranda and attachments. Primary advice letter (No. M-83-116) dated April 25, 1983 clearly contains question and conclusion sections. Includes related incoming request memoranda from April 13, 1983 and March 2, 1983, plus a 1975 FPPC opinion. Extraction focuses on the main advice letter response (M-83-116). High confidence in extraction of primary document; supporting materials appear to be reference documents.

Specifically, must loans be reported on the SEI when: (1) the loan is from a private party with check payable to the candidate who turns proceeds to campaign committee; (2) the loan is from a bank in ordinary course of business with check payable to the candidate; (3) the loan is from a private party with check payable directly to the committee; (4) the loan is from a bank with check payable directly to the committee? If the committee repays the loan, should repayment be reported on the candidate's SEI when: (1) the committee repays the lender directly; (2) the committee gives money to the candidate who repays the lender? How does whether the outstanding loan must be reported on the SEI affect whether repayments must be reported?

embedding.first_500_words: , State o\~ CaS:fornia Memorandum To Advice Request Participants Date April 25, 1983 No. M-83-116 From : PRACTICES COMMISSION Milman Subject: of Loans on SEIls In response to the question regarding disclosure of loans used for political purposes on SEI's (April 13, 1983, Advice Request Meeting, memo attached) we agreed: (1) Loans which are contributions do not need to be reported on the candidate's SEI if they are reported on the campaign statement. Section 82030(b) (1) specifically excludes contributions from income. (2) Loans which are not contributions must be reported on both the campaign statemenr-and the candidate's SEI if the candidate is personally liable on the loan. (3) Repayment of tne loan by the campaign committee, whether made directly to the lender or indirectly (to the candidate who repays the lender), need not be reported on the c and ida te 's SE I. (4) When the loan is paid off, that should be shown on the SEI (if the loan was disclosed on the SEI because it was not a contribution) • (5) A loan is not a contribution (Section 84216) if it is made by a commercial-rending institution in the ordinary course of business. All other loans (i.e., loans from private lenders, loans at below market interest, unsecured loans etc.) are presumed to be contributions, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the loan was not made for political purpose. It will usually not be possible to show that a private loan was not made for'political purposes. However, it was recognized that in some instances it may be possible to demonstrate that a loan from a private lender was not made for political purposes (i.e., from a private lender who is in the business of making loans, and makes the loan as an arms-length transaction in the ordinary course of his business), or that a loan from a bank without collateral was not made for political Advice Request Participants April 25, 1983 Page 2 purposes (if the bank ordinarily makes such loans to good customers or to get new business). Anyone asking advice about a loan, other than one from a commercial lending institution in the ordinary course of business, who thinks the loan may not be a contribution, should probably be referred to Legal for-advice. (6) For purposes of reporting and disclosure, whenever a candidate is personally liable on a loan which is not a contribution (he signs for the loan, the loan is made directly to him, or his property is used for security for the loan), the loan is income and must be reported on the SEI. embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses the reporting requirements for campaign loans on candidates' Statements of Economic Interest, distinguishing between contributions (which need not be reported on SEI if reported on campaign statements) and non-contribution loans (which must be reported on SEI if the candidate is personally liable). The letter also provides guidance on when loans qualify as contributions versus ordinary course commercial lending.
